From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

English Tory politician

For other people named Nicholas L'Estrange, see [Nicholas L'Estrange (disambiguation)](/wiki/Nicholas_L%27Estrange_(disambiguation) "Nicholas L'Estrange (disambiguation)").

**Sir Nicholas L'Estrange, 4th Baronet** (1661–1724), of [Hunstanton](/wiki/Hunstanton "Hunstanton"), [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk "Norfolk"), was an English [Tory](/wiki/Tories_(British_political_party) "Tories (British political party)") politician.[[1]](#cite_note-HOP-1)

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sir_Nicholas_L%27Estrange,_4th_Baronet&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

L'Estrange was the eldest son of Sir Nicholas L'Estrange, 3rd Baronet and his first wife, Mary, daughter of [John Coke](/w/index.php?title=John_Coke_(died_1671)&action=edit&redlink=1 "John Coke (died 1671) (page does not exist)").[[1]](#cite_note-HOP-1) An orphan from the age of 8, he was raised first by his maternal grandfather and then by [Sir Christopher Calthorpe](/wiki/Christopher_Calthorpe "Christopher Calthorpe"), the later of whom imbued L'Estrange with his high Tory views. From 1667 to 1679 L’Estrange was educated at [Christ Church, Oxford](/wiki/Christ_Church,_Oxford "Christ Church, Oxford").[[2]](#cite_note-Cokayne-2) In 1680 he was appointed a [justice of the peace](/wiki/Justice_of_the_peace "Justice of the peace") for Norfolk and the following year he was made a [deputy lieutenant](/wiki/Deputy_lieutenant "Deputy lieutenant") for the county. After the [Earl of Shaftesbury](/wiki/Anthony_Ashley_Cooper,_1st_Earl_of_Shaftesbury "Anthony Ashley Cooper, 1st Earl of Shaftesbury") planned an uprising against [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") in 1682, L'Estrange was among those who signed the loyal address to the king. In 1683 he became the colonel of a Norfolk regiment of militia.[[1]](#cite_note-HOP-1)

In 1685, he was returned to the [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") as a [Member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament "Member of Parliament") for [Castle Rising](/wiki/Castle_Rising_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Castle Rising (UK Parliament constituency)").[[2]](#cite_note-Cokayne-2) He served on one committee, on the bill for relieving imprisoned debtors. He gave negative replies on the repeal of the [Test Act](/wiki/Test_Act "Test Act") and Penal Laws, and was removed from the lieutenancy in February 1688. He did not stand for election again after the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") in 1688 and resigned all of his local offices, much to the annoyance of [Henry Howard, 7th Duke of Norfolk](/wiki/Henry_Howard,_7th_Duke_of_Norfolk "Henry Howard, 7th Duke of Norfolk").[[1]](#cite_note-HOP-1) In 1696 he was put on trial for being a [non-juror](/wiki/Nonjuring_schism "Nonjuring schism") but was acquitted on a technicality.[[1]](#cite_note-HOP-1)

He remained a non-juror and in 1721 his name was included on a list of likely [Jacobites](/wiki/Jacobitism "Jacobitism") which was sent to the [Old Pretender](/wiki/Old_Pretender "Old Pretender"). He died on 18 December 1724 and was succeeded in his title by his son, Thomas, who had converted to [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism "Roman Catholicism").[[2]](#cite_note-Cokayne-2) L'Estrange was described by [Humphrey Prideaux](/wiki/Humphrey_Prideaux "Humphrey Prideaux") as "a man of parts, virtue, and prudence. He is one of the worthiest gentlemen of the country".[[1]](#cite_note-HOP-1)
